claim: Trust in U.S. national news fell to 56% in Sept 2025, down 11 pts since March and 20 pts since 2016; under-30s trust national news (51%) about as much as social media (50%).
quote: 56% of U.S. adults now say they have a lot of or some trust in the information they get from national news organizations – down 11 percentage points since March 2025 and 20 points since 2016.

claim: 2026 Edelman Trust Barometer finds 7 in 10 people globally are hesitant or unwilling to trust those who differ in values or information sources; trust is contracting into insular circles, not flowing outward to institutions or celebrity creators.
quote: Seven in ten people globally are now hesitant or unwilling to trust those who differ from them in values, information sources, or backgrounds. Broad broadcast messaging is losing its grip.

claim: Cambridge Analytica built personality profiles for 100M+ U.S. voters via a Facebook personality app that harvested data from friends-of-friends; Facebook was fined $5B by the FTC in 2019.
quote: Facebook was fined $5 billion by the Federal Trade Commission in July 2019 due to its privacy violations.

claim: Stanford GSB analysis: psychometric microtargeting science was thinner than claimed, but the public lesson — 'you are the product' — durably eroded trust in tech platforms.
quote: The most lasting impact of the scandal is the erosion of blind trust in technology... the idea that 'you are the product' became a cultural wake-up call.

claim: Frances Haugen testified that Facebook's engagement-ranked feed amplifies rage, hate, and misinformation because those signals predict longer sessions.
quote: It is causing teenagers to be exposed to more anorexia content. It is pulling families apart. And in places like Ethiopia, it's literally fanning ethnic violence.

claim: MIT Tech Review summary: engagement-based ranking systematically privileges sensational content because rage and outrage produce more clicks than empathy.
quote: The engagement-based formula helps sensational content, such as posts that feature rage, hate or misinformation, travel far and wide.

claim: The global attention economy is projected to top $700B by 2025, with social media accounting for >1/3; algorithms privileging outrage are a feature, not a bug, of ad-funded scale.
quote: By 2025, it is projected to top $700 billion, with social media alone accounting for more than a third of that sum.

claim: Tristan Harris (Center for Humane Technology) frames ad-funded platforms as a race to the bottom of the brain stem; his April 2025 TED talk extends the same argument to AI.
quote: In April 2025, Harris delivered a TED Talk titled, 'Why AI is our ultimate test and greatest invitation.' He calls on society to learn from the mistakes of social media's rollout.

claim: Goodhart's law: when a measure becomes a target, it stops measuring what it was meant to. Engagement metrics are the textbook case — once they pay rent, content optimises for clicks rather than meaning.
quote: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.

claim: DarkBench (ICLR 2025) finds dark patterns appear in 48% of LLM outputs across 660 prompts; 'sneaking' shows up in 79% of conversations, brand bias is widespread (Meta models prefer Llama, etc.).
quote: On aggregate, dark patterns appeared in 48% of model outputs... the most commonly occurring dark pattern was sneaking, which appeared in 79% of conversations.

claim: VentureBeat coverage: DarkBench's six categories are sycophancy, brand bias, user retention, anthropomorphism, harmful generation, sneaking; risks rise as LLMs incorporate ads.
quote: Sycophancy and other dark patterns like brand bias may soon rise, especially as LLMs begin to incorporate advertising and e-commerce.

claim: 'Ads that Talk Back' study: 49% of participants failed to notice ads in chatbot responses even when disclosure labels were present; once disclosed, ads were rated manipulative and less trustworthy.
quote: 49.15% of participants failed to notice ads even with disclosure labels present... 22 participants reported their trust was negatively impacted by ad presence.

claim: Tom's Hardware (Dec 2025): OpenAI is reportedly moving toward giving sponsored content preferential treatment inside ChatGPT answers, using chat history for personalization.
quote: Sponsored content could allegedly be given preferential treatment in LLM's responses, OpenAI to use chat data to deliver highly personalized results.

claim: OpenAI began rolling Sponsored Suggestions into the live ChatGPT experience on 9 Feb 2026; ads sit at the bottom of the chat and are matched to conversational intent.
quote: OpenAI announced its first native advertising format for ChatGPT in January 2026 and officially began rolling Sponsored Suggestions into the live experience on February 9, 2026.

claim: Ipsos (Jan 2026): 63% of U.S. adults agree ads in AI search results would make them trust the results less; only 36% think ads would simplify shopping.
quote: 63% of adults surveyed either strongly (27%) or somewhat (36%) agreed that having ads in AI search results will make them trust the results less.

claim: Perplexity pulled all advertising in Feb 2026 across all tiers, citing trust erosion; executives said ads risk making users 'suspicious of everything'.
quote: Perplexity completely eliminated advertising for all users regardless of subscription tier, betting that maintaining universal trust provides better long-term competitive advantage.

claim: AI monetization is splitting: Perplexity and Anthropic stay ad-free; OpenAI and Google integrate ads. Reach-first vs trust-first is now an explicit market segmentation.
quote: The divergent approaches—Perplexity and Anthropic's Claude remaining ad-free while OpenAI and Google integrate advertising—represent competing hypotheses about user preferences.

claim: 56% of social media users see AI slop often or very often; 50% of Gen Z have muted or blocked a brand because its content felt like slop.
quote: 56% of social media respondents reported seeing AI slop on social media often or very often, with 83% seeing it at least sometimes. Half of Gen Z respondents said they had muted or blocked a brand or creator because their content felt like AI slop.

claim: 'Slop' was named Word of the Year by Macquarie Dictionary, Merriam-Webster, and the American Dialect Society in late 2025 — a cultural marker that the feed itself is felt as untrustworthy.
quote: In December 2025, the Macquarie Dictionary, Merriam-Webster, and the American Dialect Society named 'slop' as the Word of the Year.

claim: Fraudlogix 2026: 20.64% of programmatic ad impressions in 2025 showed invalid-traffic signals — ~$37B at risk in U.S. programmatic spend alone.
quote: Of the 105.7 billion impressions in our dataset, 21.81 billion (20.64%) showed risk signals indicating invalid traffic... $180 billion × 20.64% = $37.15 billion.

claim: 32.5% of U.S. internet users run ad blockers (Q2 2025, GWI); ~42.7% globally use ad-blocking on at least one device. The ad-funded contract is being opted out of at scale.
quote: As of Q2 2025, GWI estimates that 32.5% of Americans use ad blockers... 42.7% of internet users utilize ad-blocking tools on at least one device.

claim: Banner blindness affects ~86% of consumers; display ad CTRs sit around 0.05%. The ad supply has scaled past the eyeball it was sold against.
quote: On average, 86% of consumers experience banner blindness... display advertisement click-through rates average just 0.05%.

claim: EU Digital Fairness Act proposal expected Q4 2026; targets dark patterns, addictive design, and unfair personalisation that exploits consumer vulnerabilities.
quote: The Commission will table the DFA in late 2026... The DFA will specifically address problematic personalisation practices, including situations where consumer vulnerabilities are targeted for the purposes of personalised advertising and pricing.

claim: FTC continues treating dark patterns as deceptive practice under Section 5; AI personalisation is shifting consumer-protection focus from fraud to the architecture of choice itself.
quote: Consumer protection risks are shifting from traditional fraud to the very architecture of digital choice.

claim: AI lets dark-pattern UX be A/B-tested at micro-resolution: systems detect that a red button lifts clicks 0.4% and ship the variant before a designer has reviewed it.
quote: AI-powered UX systems test millions of micro-changes and know if a red button makes 0.4% more clicks.

claim: Naturalistic experiments on YouTube (~9,000 participants, 2025 PNAS) found short-term filter-bubble exposure produced limited polarization effects, contesting the strongest rabbit-hole framing.
quote: Short-term exposure to filter-bubble recommendation systems has limited polarization effects: Naturalistic experiments on YouTube.
